chapters.title: Tutorial: Command-line Programs, Files, Images, HTTP, and Servers
chapters.description: echo, dup, animated GIFs, fetching URLs, concurrent fetch, and handlers

chapters.title: Program Structure
chapters.description: names, declarations, variables, assignment, type declarations, packages, files, and scope

chapters.title: Basic Data Types
chapters.description: integers, floats, complex numbers, booleans, strings, constants, bytes, and runes

chapters.title: Composite Types
chapters.description: arrays, slices, maps, structs, JSON, templates, and graph-shaped data

chapters.title: Functions
chapters.description: function signatures, recursion, multiple returns, errors, function values, closures, variadic functions, defer, panic, and recover

chapters.title: Methods
chapters.description: method declarations, pointer receivers, nil receivers, struct embedding, method values, and encapsulation

chapters.title: Interfaces
chapters.description: interfaces as contracts, io.Reader/io.Writer, sorting, type assertions, type switches, and XML/HTML token streams

chapters.title: Goroutines and Channels
chapters.description: goroutines, channels, pipelines, cancellation, multiplexing, chat servers, and concurrency patterns

chapters.title: Shared Variables and Mutexes
chapters.description: race conditions, sync.Mutex, sync.RWMutex, memory synchronization, lazy initialization, and confinement

chapters.title: Packages and the Go Tool
chapters.description: package organization, import paths, modules, documentation, internal packages, go list, go test, and vendoring

chapters.title: Testing
chapters.description: test functions, table tests, random testing, coverage, benchmarks, profiling, and examples

chapters.title: Reflection
chapters.description: reflect.Type, reflect.Value, display functions, struct tags, S-expressions, and dynamic value inspection

chapters.title: Low-Level Programming
chapters.description: unsafe.Sizeof, Alignof, Offsetof, unsafe.Pointer, cgo, and when low-level tricks are justified

problems.title: Exercise 1.1: Program Name and Args
problems.statement: Given a program name and command-line args, return a slice whose first element is the program name followed by all args.
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns args, strings, cli into a deterministic Solve function checked by local tests. The target behavior is: Given a program name and command-line args, return a slice whose first element is the program name followed by all args.

- Use a slice to preserve command-line order; the program name is just another string value at index 0.
- Strings are immutable byte sequences; choose helpers like HasPrefix, Join, Split, or builders when the task is about text shape.
- Keep command-line behavior separate from the pure transformation so it can be tested with ordinary values.
problems.approach:
- Start in the editor from the starter code for exercise 1.1.
- Keep `func Solve(program string, args []string) []string` compatible with the tests.
- Use the examples to check the result shape before pressing Submit.
- When the implementation is deterministic, Submit runs the local tests from this exercise.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.

Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Given a program name and command-line args, return a slice whose first element is the program name followed by all args.

problems.title: Exercise 1.4: Duplicate Lines With File Names
problems.statement: Given filename-to-lines data, return duplicated lines mapped to sorted file names in which they occur.
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns files, maps into a deterministic Solve function checked by local tests. The target behavior is: Given filename-to-lines data, return duplicated lines mapped to sorted file names in which they occur.

- Model file contents explicitly, then group or count lines before printing or returning the result.
- Use a map when the exercise asks you to count, group, deduplicate, or look up by key.
problems.approach:
- Start in the editor from the starter code for exercise 1.4.
- Keep `func Solve(files map[string][]string) map[string][]string` compatible with the tests.
- Use the examples to check the result shape before pressing Submit.
- When the implementation is deterministic, Submit runs the local tests from this exercise.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.
problems.explanation: Exercise 1.4 is about files, maps. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Given filename-to-lines data, return duplicated lines mapped to sorted file names in which they occur.
problems.solutionCode:
import "sort"
func Solve(files map[string][]string) map[string][]string { seen:=map[string]map[string]bool{}; for name,lines:=range files { for _,line:=range lines { if seen[line]==nil {seen[line]=map[string]bool{}}; seen[line][name]=true } }; out:=map[string][]string{}; for line,set:=range seen { if len(set)>1 { for f:=range set {out[line]=append(out[line],f)}; sort.Strings(out[line]) } }; return out }
problems.howItWorks:
This exercise turns files, maps into a deterministic Solve function checked by local tests. The target behavior is: Given filename-to-lines data, return duplicated lines mapped to sorted file names in which they occur.

- Model file contents explicitly, then group or count lines before printing or returning the result.
- Use a map when the exercise asks you to count, group, deduplicate, or look up by key.
problems.syntax:

- Core shape: `func Solve(files map[string][]string) map[string][]string`.
- Keep the `Solve` signature unchanged; the local judge calls that exact function.
- Likely import pattern: `import "sort"`.
- `bufio.Scanner` reads line-oriented files; maps can collect which file produced each line.
- `m := map[K]V{}` then `m[key] = value` stores grouped or counted data.

problems.title: Exercise 1.8: Ensure HTTP Prefix

func Solve(url string) string { if strings.HasPrefix(url,"http://")||strings.HasPrefix(url,"https://") {return url}; return "http://"+url }

problems.title: Exercise 1.10: web site that produces large
problems.statement: Find a web site that produces a large amount of data. Investigate caching by running fetchall twice in succession to see whether the reported time changes much. Do you get the same content each time? Adapt fetchall to print its output to a file so it can be examined.

problems.title: Exercise 1.12: Parse Lissajous Params
problems.statement: Given query parameters, return cycles with default 5 when missing or invalid.
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns http, strconv, defaults into a deterministic Solve function checked by local tests. The target behavior is: Given query parameters, return cycles with default 5 when missing or invalid.

- Separate URL/request setup from response processing so redirects, prefixes, headers, and bodies are easy to reason about.
- Convert strings at the boundary and decide a fallback for invalid values.
- Read optional settings defensively and use clear defaults when input is missing or invalid.
problems.approach:
- Start in the editor from the starter code for exercise 1.12.
- Keep `func Solve(params map[string]string) int` compatible with the tests.
- Use the examples to check the result shape before pressing Submit.
- When the implementation is deterministic, Submit runs the local tests from this exercise.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.
Keep a command or sample input that proves your solution works.
problems.explanation: Exercise 1.12 is about http, strconv, defaults. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Given query parameters, return cycles with default 5 when missing or invalid.
problems.solutionCode:
import "strconv"
func Solve(params map[string]string) int { v,err:=strconv.Atoi(params["cycles"]); if err!=nil || v<=0 {return 5}; return v }
problems.howItWorks:

- Read optional settings defensively and use clear defaults when input is missing or invalid.

problems.title: Exercise 3.4: Following the approach of the
problems.statement: Following the approach of the Lissajous example in Section 1.7, construct a web server that computes surfaces and writes SVG data to the client. The server must set the Con- tent-Type header like this: w.Header().Set("Content-Type", "image/svg+xml") (This step was not required in the Lissajous example because the server uses standard heuristics to recognize common formats like PNG from the first 512 bytes of the response and generates the proper header.) Allow the client to specify values like height, width, and color as HTTP request parameters.
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns http, server, lissajous, xml into a project-style program verified by a reproducible run. The target behavior is: Following the approach of the Lissajous example in Section 1.7, construct a web server that computes surfaces and writes SVG data to the client.

- Separate URL/request setup from response processing so redirects, prefixes, headers, and bodies are easy to reason about.
- HTTP handlers receive a request, write a response, and should keep shared state explicit.
- The animation is a sequence of palette indexes; changing colors means changing palette values or how indexes are selected.
- XML tasks are about element names, attributes, and nested structure mapped to Go values.
problems.approach:
- Build the full program or experiment for exercise 3.4; project entries are not hidden-test puzzles.
- Use Run mode or your local terminal to execute the smallest version that demonstrates the behavior.
- Record the command, sample input or file, and observed output as your proof.

problems.title: Exercise 3.8: Rendering fractals at high zoom
problems.statement: Rendering fractals at high zoom levels demands great arithmetic precision. Implement the same fractal using four different representations of numbers: complex64, com- plex128, big.Float, and big.Rat. (The latter two types are found in the math/big package. Float uses arbitrar y but bounded-precision floating-point; Rat uses unbounded-precision rational numbers.) How do they compare in performance and memory usage? At what zoom levels do rendering artifacts become visible?

problems.explanation: Exercise 3.10 is about strings. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Insert commas in a non-negative integer string.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(s string) string { n:=len(s); if n<=3{return s}; r:=n%3; out:=""; if r>0 {out=s[:r]; if r<n {out+=","}}; for i:=r;i<n;i+=3 {out+=s[i:i+3]; if i+3<n {out+=","}}; return out }

problems.explanation: Exercise 3.12 is about strings, runes, maps. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Return true if two strings contain the same runes with the same counts.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(a,b string) bool { m:=map[rune]int{}; for _,r:=range a {m[r]++}; for _,r:=range b {m[r]--}; for _,v:=range m { if v!=0 {return false} }; return true }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("listen","silent"),true); assertEqual(Solve("go","goo"),false); assertEqual(Solve("夜猫","猫夜"),true) }

examples.output: true

problems.solutionCode:

Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Replace each run of Unicode whitespace with a single ASCII space.
problems.solutionCode:
import "unicode"
func Solve(s string) string { out:=[]rune{}; inSpace:=false; for _,r:=range s { if unicode.IsSpace(r) { if !inSpace {out=append(out,' '); inSpace=true} } else {out=append(out,r); inSpace=false} }; return string(out) }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve("a\t\n b"),"a b"); assertEqual(Solve("x  y"),"x y") }

problems.solutionCode:
import "unicode"
func Solve(s string) map[string]int { m:=map[string]int{"letters":0,"digits":0,"spaces":0,"other":0}; for _,r:=range s { switch {case unicode.IsLetter(r): m["letters"]++; case unicode.IsDigit(r): m["digits"]++; case unicode.IsSpace(r): m["spaces"]++; default: m["other"]++} }; return m }

problems.title: Exercise 5.10: Topological Sort Order
problems.statement: Given prerequisite edges course -> prereq, return a deterministic topological order with prerequisites before courses.
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns maps, sort, graphs into a deterministic Solve function checked by local tests. The target behavior is: Given prerequisite edges course -> prereq, return a deterministic topological order with prerequisites before courses.

- Use a map when the exercise asks you to count, group, deduplicate, or look up by key.
- Sort only after collecting all comparable values; define a less function when natural order is not enough.
- Represent relationships explicitly, then traverse from known starting points.
problems.approach:
- Start in the editor from the starter code for exercise 5.10.
- Keep `func Solve(prereqs map[string][]string) []string` compatible with the tests.
- Use the examples to check the result shape before pressing Submit.
- When the implementation is deterministic, Submit runs the local tests from this exercise.
pitfalls: Changing too much of the original example at once.
Ignoring errors because the happy path worked once.
Treating open-ended book exercises like single-output puzzles instead of reproducible programs.
hints: Find the chapter example this exercise modifies.
Make the smallest visible change first.

problems.explanation: Exercise 5.10 is about maps, sort, graphs. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Given prerequisite edges course -> prereq, return a deterministic topological order with prerequisites before courses.
problems.solutionCode:
import "sort"
func Solve(prereqs map[string][]string) []string { seen:=map[string]bool{}; out:=[]string{}; var visit func(string); visit=func(x string){ if seen[x]{return}; seen[x]=true; ps:=append([]string{},prereqs[x]...); sort.Strings(ps); for _,p:=range ps {visit(p)}; out=append(out,x)}; keys:=[]string{}; for k:=range prereqs {keys=append(keys,k); for _,p:=range prereqs[k] { if _,ok:=prereqs[p]; !ok {prereqs[p]=nil} } }; keys=keys[:0]; for k:=range prereqs {keys=append(keys,k)}; sort.Strings(keys); for _,k:=range keys {visit(k)}; return out }
problems.testCode: func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(map[string][]string{"algorithms":{"data structures"},"calculus":{"linear algebra"}}),[]string{"data structures","algorithms","linear algebra","calculus"}) }

examples.output: [data structures, algorithms]

- Use a map when the exercise asks you to count, group, deduplicate, or look up by key.
- Sort only after collecting all comparable values; define a less function when natural order is not enough.
- Represent relationships explicitly, then traverse from known starting points.

problems.solutionCode: func Solve(nums ...int) (int,int) { mn,mx:=nums[0],nums[0]; for _,x:=range nums[1:] { if x<mn {mn=x}; if x>mx {mx=x} }; return mn,mx }

problems.title: Exercise 6.1: IntSet Len Remove Clear Copy
problems.statement: For a set represented as []int, return sorted unique elements after removing deletes.
problems.lesson:

problems.explanation: Exercise 6.1 is about methods, sets. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: For a set represented as []int, return sorted unique elements after removing deletes.
problems.solutionCode:
import "sort"
func Solve(adds,deletes []int) []int { m:=map[int]bool{}; for _,x:=range adds {m[x]=true}; for _,x:=range deletes {delete(m,x)}; out:=[]int{}; for x:=range m {out=append(out,x)}; sort.Ints(out); return out }

problems.title: Exercise 6.5: type of each word used
problems.statement: The type of each word used by IntSet is uint64, but 64-bit arithmetic may be inefficient on a 32-bit platform. Adapt the program to use the uint type, which is the most efficient unsigned integer type for the platform. Instead of dividing by 64, define a constant holding the effective size of uint in bits, 32 or 64. You can use the perhaps too-clever expres- sion 32 << (^uint(0) >> 63) for this purpose.

problems.explanation: Exercise 8.1 is about channels, time. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Given HH:MM:SS strings, return them sorted as seconds since midnight.
problems.solutionCode:
import ("sort"; "strconv"; "strings")
func Solve(times []string) []int { out:=[]int{}; for _,t:=range times { p:=strings.Split(t,":"); if len(p)!=3 {continue}; h,_:=strconv.Atoi(p[0]); m,_:=strconv.Atoi(p[1]); s,_:=strconv.Atoi(p[2]); out=append(out,h*3600+m*60+s) }; sort.Ints(out); return out }

problems.title: Exercise 8.3: In netcat3 the interface value
problems.statement: In netcat3, the interface value conn has the concrete type *net.TCPConn, which represents a TCP connection. A TCP connection consists of two halves that may be closed independently using its CloseRead and CloseBuild methods. Adapt the main goroutine of netcat3 to close only the write half of the connection so that the program will continue to print the final echoes from the reverb1 server even after the standard input has been closed. (Doing this for the reverb2 server is harder; see Exercise 8.4.)

problems.title: Exercise 8.10: HTTP requests may be cancelled
problems.statement: HTTP requests may be cancelled by closing the optional Cancel channel in the http.Request struct. Adapt the web crawler of Section 8.6 to support cancellation. Hint: the http.Get convenience function does not give you an opportunity to customize a Request. Instead, create the request using http.NewRequest, set its Cancel field, then per- form the request by calling http.DefaultClient.Do(req).
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns http, channel into a project-style program verified by a reproducible run. The target behavior is: HTTP requests may be cancelled by closing the optional Cancel channel in the http.Request struct.

- Separate URL/request setup from response processing so redirects, prefixes, headers, and bodies are easy to reason about.
- Channels communicate values and can also coordinate completion.

problems.title: Exercise 8.15: Failure of any client program
problems.statement: Failure of any client program to read data in a timely manner ultimately causes all clients to get stuck. Adapt the broadcaster to skip a message rather than wait if a client writer is not ready to accept it. Alternatively, add buffering to each client’s outgoing message channel so that most messages are not dropped; the broadcaster should use a non-blocking send to this channel.
problems.lesson:

- Channels communicate values and can also coordinate completion.

problems.solutionCode:
import ("sort"; "strings")
func Solve(paths []string) map[string][]string { out:=map[string][]string{"std":{},"external":{}}; for _,p:=range paths { if strings.Contains(strings.Split(p,"/")[0],".") {out["external"]=append(out["external"],p)} else {out["std"]=append(out["std"],p)} }; sort.Strings(out["std"]); sort.Strings(out["external"]); return out }

problems.solutionCode:
import "unicode"
func Solve(s string) map[string]int { m:=map[string]int{"letters":0,"digits":0,"spaces":0}; for _,r:=range s { if unicode.IsLetter(r){m["letters"]++}; if unicode.IsDigit(r){m["digits"]++}; if unicode.IsSpace(r){m["spaces"]++} }; return m }

problems.solutionCode:
func rev(xs []int){ for i,j:=0,len(xs)-1;i<j;i,j=i+1,j-1 {xs[i],xs[j]=xs[j],xs[i]} }
func Solve(xs []int) []int { out:=append([]int{},xs...); rev(out); rev(out); return out }

problems.explanation: Exercise 12.6 is about reflection, structs. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Return exported field names of a struct value in declaration order.
problems.solutionCode:
import "reflect"
func Solve(v any) []string { t:=reflect.TypeOf(v); if t==nil {return nil}; if t.Kind()==reflect.Pointer {t=t.Elem()}; if t.Kind()!=reflect.Struct {return nil}; out:=[]string{}; for i:=0;i<t.NumField();i++ { f:=t.Field(i); if f.PkgPath=="" {out=append(out,f.Name)} }; return out }
problems.testCode:
type User struct{Name string; age int; Email string}
func main(){ assertEqual(Solve(User{}),[]string{"Name","Email"}); assertEqual(Solve(3),[]string(nil)) }

examples.output: [Name, Email]

problems.explanation: Exercise 13.2 is about unsafe, alignment. Instead of memorizing a finished answer, read the required behavior, identify the input and output values, and build the smallest Go implementation that proves this specific task: Round a size up to the next multiple of alignment.
problems.solutionCode: func Solve(size,align uintptr) uintptr { if align==0 {return size}; r:=size%align; if r==0 {return size}; return size+(align-r) }
problems.howItWorks:
This exercise turns unsafe, alignment into a deterministic Solve function checked by local tests. The target behavior is: Round a size up to the next multiple of alignment.

- Unsafe code bypasses type safety, so the exercise is about understanding representation boundaries.
- Alignment rounds addresses or sizes up to a multiple required by the target type.
problems.syntax:
- Exercise 13.2 syntax focus: use the patterns below for this specific task.
- Core shape: `func Solve(size, align uintptr) uintptr`.
- Keep the `Solve` signature unchanged; the local judge calls that exact function.
- `unsafe.Sizeof`, `unsafe.Alignof`, and pointer conversion require exact representation reasoning.
- `rounded := size + (align - size%align)` is the usual round-up shape when a remainder exists.
problems.problemText: Round a size up to the next multiple of alignment.

problems.title: Exercise 13.3: sync Mutex to make bzip2
problems.statement: Practice using sync.Mutex to make bzip2.writer safe for concurrent use by multiple goroutines.
problems.lesson:
This exercise turns writer, goroutine, mutex into a project-style program verified by a reproducible run. The target behavior is: Practice using sync.Mutex to make bzip2.writer safe for concurrent use by multiple goroutines.

- io.Writer accepts bytes through Write, so producers should report errors from the write boundary.
- A goroutine runs concurrently, so the result must be synchronized or sent somewhere safe.
- A mutex protects shared state by making read/write critical sections explicit.
